subject: it
greeting: Hey! Glad you're putting in the work for IT — those marks won't earn themselves. Quick reminder: I'm Kairu, the Student Hub AI. AI can slip up, so always double-check tricky stuff with your IT teacher or class notes. Now let's go!
note: Questions transcribed verbatim from real CSEC IT Paper 1 past papers. Order, stems, and option positions preserved as in the original. Explanations and distractor analysis written by Kairu — Student Hub's AI tutor.

questions.stem: Which part of the central processing unit (CPU) coordinates the activities of the entire unit?
questions.explanation: The Control Unit (CU) directs the operations of the CPU. It tells the ALU and registers what to do, when to do it, and where to send the results. It is the conductor of the orchestra.

distractors.A: Registers are tiny, fast storage locations within the CPU — they hold data being worked on, but they don't coordinate anything.
distractors.C: Main memory (RAM) holds data and instructions for the CPU but is outside the CPU itself.
distractors.D: The ALU performs arithmetic and logic operations — it does the work, but it doesn't coordinate the rest.

questions.stem: Which of the following is true about the arithmetic and logic unit (ALU)?

options.C: Is also called memory or main memory
options.D: Sequentially accesses and decodes program instructions

questions.explanation: The Arithmetic Logic Unit performs all arithmetic operations (add, subtract, multiply, divide) and all logical comparisons (greater than, less than, equal to, AND, OR, NOT). The clue is in its name.

distractors.A: Permanent storage is the job of secondary storage devices like hard drives, not the ALU.
distractors.C: Main memory is RAM — a separate component from the ALU.
distractors.D: Decoding and sequencing instructions is the job of the Control Unit, not the ALU.

questions.stem: Which of the following can be termed 'application software'?
questions.explanation: Microsoft Edge is a web browser — application software that performs a specific task for the user. The other three are operating systems, which are system software.

distractors.A: DOS (Disk Operating System) is system software — it manages the computer's resources.
distractors.B: Linux is an operating system — system software.
distractors.C: Mac OS (now macOS) is Apple's operating system — system software.

questions.stem: Which of the following activities would NOT be considered cloud storage?

options.B: Data stored and accessible on a LAN
options.C: Data backup and storage devices on the Internet
options.D: Data managed by storage providers or hosting companies

questions.explanation: Cloud storage means data held on remote servers and accessed over the Internet. A LAN is a private local network, not the cloud — so storing data on a LAN is local storage, not cloud storage.

distractors.A: Remote servers accessed over the Internet — that's the textbook definition of cloud storage.
distractors.C: Internet-based backup and storage IS cloud storage.
distractors.D: Third-party storage providers (Google Drive, Dropbox, AWS) are exactly what cloud storage means.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT included in cabled transmission media?
questions.explanation: Satellite communication is wireless — it uses microwaves transmitted through space, no cables involved. Coaxial, fibre optic, and twisted-pair cables are all physical wires.

distractors.A: Coaxial cable (used in cable TV and older networks) is a cabled medium.
distractors.C: Fibre optic cable transmits data as pulses of light through glass — definitely cabled.
distractors.D: Twisted pair cable (e.g., Ethernet/Cat 5/6) is the most common cabled networking medium.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows the basic operations of a general-purpose computer system: W → X → Y, with Z connected. Which of the following functions is carried out in box Z?
questions.explanation: The four basic operations of a computer are Input → Process → Output → Storage. In the standard system diagram: W = Input, X = Processing, Y = Output, and Z = Storage (connected to processing for saving and retrieving data).

distractors.A: Input is the W stage — getting data into the system from devices like keyboards.
distractors.B: Output is the Y stage — sending results to devices like monitors and printers.
distractors.D: Processing is X — the CPU operates here.

diagram.alt: Computer system diagram with boxes W, X, Y in a horizontal flow and Z connected to processing

questions.stem: The RP Secondary School has two campuses in different locations and wants the administrative staff to have access to all student and teacher data. Which of the following networks would be MOST suitable to enable such access?
questions.explanation: Two campuses in different locations means data has to travel a wide geographical distance. WAN (Wide Area Network) is designed to connect networks across long distances — different cities, countries, or continents.

distractors.A: A LAN covers a single building or small site — too small to span two separate campuses.
distractors.C: A MAN (Metropolitan Area Network) covers a single city. If the campuses are in the same city it might work, but WAN is more general and 'most suitable' here.
distractors.D: A WLAN is just a wireless LAN — same range limitation as a LAN.

questions.stem: For which of the following interface types is the user required to remember specific instructions and syntax?
questions.explanation: A command-driven (command-line) interface requires the user to type exact commands and remember the right syntax (e.g., cd /home/user, rm -f file.txt). One typo and the command fails.

distractors.A: Menu-driven interfaces present options for the user to pick from — no memorisation needed.
distractors.B: GUIs use icons, windows, and pointing — visual, not memorisation-based.
distractors.C: There's no standard interface called 'computer-screen' — this option is a distractor.

questions.stem: The device used for printing industrial quality architectural drawings is the
questions.explanation: Plotters are designed for large-format, precise vector drawings — exactly what architectural and engineering plans require. They use pens or ink jets that move along defined paths to produce sharp, scalable line drawings.

distractors.B: A scanner is an INPUT device — it reads images, not prints them.
distractors.C: Laser printers are excellent for documents and standard graphics, but limited in size and not optimised for the precise vector lines of architectural drawings.
distractors.D: Dot matrix printers are old, low-quality, and very limited in resolution — not suitable for industrial-quality output.

questions.stem: Which of the following devices is used to input data into the computer?
questions.explanation: A mouse sends position and click data to the computer — a clear input device. The other three options are either output (monitor) or storage (flash drives, external hard drives).

distractors.B: A monitor displays output from the computer — it does not send data into the computer.
distractors.C: A USB flash drive is a storage device — it can be both an input source and an output destination, but its primary classification is storage, not 'input device' as the question asks.
distractors.D: An external hard drive is also a storage device, not an input device.

questions.stem: Which of the following countermeasures can ONLY be implemented as a software measure to mitigate the effects of identified threats to a computer system?
questions.explanation: Encryption is fundamentally an algorithmic/mathematical process — it scrambles data using software keys and ciphers. Although hardware can accelerate it, encryption itself is implemented in software. The other measures all have hardware variants.

distractors.A: Firewalls come in BOTH software (e.g., Windows Defender Firewall) AND hardware (dedicated appliances) forms.
distractors.C: Backup and recovery typically uses both software (backup utilities) and hardware (tape drives, external disks).
distractors.D: Intrusion detection systems can be software-based or dedicated hardware appliances.

questions.stem: Unauthorized, real-time interception of a private communication on a computer network is referred to as
questions.explanation: Electronic eavesdropping means listening in on or capturing communications as they travel across a network — without authorisation, in real time. Examples include packet sniffing, wiretapping, and Wi-Fi snooping.

distractors.A: Propaganda is the spread of biased information to influence opinion — not interception of communications.
distractors.B: Identity theft is stealing personal details to impersonate someone — not specifically intercepting live communications.
distractors.C: Industrial espionage is the theft of business secrets, often via varied means; eavesdropping is the more precise term for the activity described.

questions.stem: Double-entry data verification involves

options.B: the data entered being copied to another device
options.C: different data being entered once by two different persons
options.D: the same data being entered once by one person and then re-entered by another person

questions.explanation: Double-entry verification means the same data is keyed in twice — typically by two different people. The system compares the two entries and flags any difference, catching typos before they become permanent records.

distractors.A: Single entry — there's no double, so no verification.
distractors.B: Copying isn't verification; it's just duplication of whatever was entered (mistakes and all).
distractors.C: Different data entered once doesn't catch errors — there's nothing to compare against.

questions.stem: Part of a private network using Internet technology and public telecommunications that can be accessed by business suppliers, vendors, customers and partners is called
questions.explanation: An extranet extends a private intranet to authorised external parties — suppliers, partners, and customers — over the Internet. It's like an intranet with a controlled door for outsiders.

distractors.A: The Internet is fully public — not private at all.
distractors.B: An intranet is private and restricted to people within the organisation only.
distractors.D: Ethernet is a networking technology (cabling and protocols), not a type of network with a specific user-base.

questions.stem: Data was captured using a data-capture form. Which of the following checks should be used to ensure that the data captured matches the source data?
questions.explanation: A visual check is when a human reads the captured data and compares it to the original source document — the most direct way to confirm a match. The others are validation checks for sensible data, not for matching the source.

distractors.A: Range checks ensure values fall in an allowed range (e.g., months 1–12) — they don't check against the source.
distractors.C: Data type checks ensure entries are the right type (number vs text) — but a number that's the wrong number still passes.
distractors.D: Consistency checks compare related fields (e.g., end date is after start date) — they don't compare to the source document.

questions.stem: Which of the following functions is performed by a modem?

options.C: Prevents analog and digital signals from being uploaded
options.D: Prevents analog and digital signals from being downloaded

questions.explanation: Modem stands for MOdulator-DEModulator. It modulates digital signals (from your computer) into analog signals (for the phone or cable line) and demodulates incoming analog signals back into digital — making communication possible between your digital device and the analog network.

distractors.B: Modems convert signals; they don't merely regulate them.
distractors.C: A modem doesn't prevent uploads — it enables both directions of communication.
distractors.D: A modem doesn't prevent downloads either — that's the job of a firewall or filter.

questions.stem: Which of the following is true of an intranet?

options.D: Offers limited access to employees and suppliers

questions.explanation: An intranet is a private network restricted to an organisation's employees. Allowing supplier access would extend it into an extranet.

distractors.A: Intranets are deliberately restricted, not unlimited — that's the whole point.
distractors.B: Allowing suppliers makes it an extranet, not an intranet.
distractors.D: Including suppliers extends it beyond an intranet — this describes an extranet.

questions.stem: Transferring data from your computer to a remote computer is referred to as
questions.explanation: Uploading = sending data FROM your computer TO a remote one (you push content up to a server). Downloading = the reverse direction.

distractors.A: Browsing is moving between web pages — not specifically a file transfer.
distractors.C: Data mining is the analysis of large datasets to find patterns — not transferring files.
distractors.D: Downloading is the opposite direction — pulling data from a remote computer to yours.

questions.stem: Which of the following types of technologies is used by banks to read data on cheques?
questions.explanation: MICR (Magnetic Ink Character Recognition) reads the special magnetic-ink digits printed at the bottom of cheques — the routing number and account number. The magnetic property makes them readable by the bank's machines even when the cheque is dirty or marked.

distractors.A: POS (Point Of Sale) refers to checkout terminals that read credit/debit cards — not cheques.
distractors.B: OCR reads ordinary printed or typed characters as text — too unreliable for the financial security of cheques.
distractors.C: OMR (Optical Mark Recognition) reads marks like filled-in bubbles on multiple-choice answer sheets — different application.

questions.stem: The following information applies to this and the next question: Customers log in to an online application using a username and an encrypted password to submit a record of their monthly meter reading to a utility company. Which file organisation method is used for the filing of all the records submitted by customers online?
questions.explanation: Customer meter records arrive in random order online but the utility needs to retrieve them efficiently by account number for billing. Index-sequential organisation stores records sequentially while keeping an index for fast direct lookup — the best of both worlds.

distractors.A: Serial files store records in the order they arrive, with no key — fine for storage but slow to search.
distractors.B: Random/direct files allow fast lookup but lose any natural ordering, useful but less efficient for batch processing of all records.
distractors.C: Pure sequential files store records in key order — fine for batch processing but require sorting on input.

questions.stem: Usernames and encrypted passwords are used to authenticate customers when they are logging into their accounts. Which file access method is the MOST efficient access method to use to authenticate customers when they are keying into their accounts?
questions.explanation: Direct (also called random) access lets the system jump straight to the user's record using the username as the key. No need to scan through every record sequentially — instant authentication, even with millions of users.

distractors.B: Serial access reads records in storage order from the start — it would have to scan past every other user's record first. Slow.
distractors.C: Random access is essentially the same idea as direct access in many contexts; here Direct is the more standard term used in CSEC pedagogy for this lookup pattern.
distractors.D: Sequential access reads in key order — still has to scan through; not optimal for instant authentication.

questions.stem: In order to use a program stored on a secondary storage device, the program MUST be transferred
questions.explanation: The CPU can only execute instructions that are in RAM (primary memory). When you run a program, the operating system loads it from the hard drive (secondary storage) into RAM first.

distractors.A: ROM is read-only and stores firmware (e.g., BIOS) — programs aren't loaded into ROM at runtime.
distractors.C: A flash drive is just another secondary storage device — not the place programs run from.
distractors.D: An optical disk (CD/DVD) is also secondary storage — programs run from RAM, not directly from optical disks.

questions.stem: Records on magnetic tape are stored
questions.explanation: Magnetic tape is a linear medium — the read/write head must pass through every record in order to reach a target one. This is sequential access. To find record 10000, you must wind past records 1–9999.

distractors.A: Direct access requires being able to jump to any location instantly — tape can't do that.
distractors.B: Random access likewise requires jumping; tape is strictly linear.
distractors.D: Index-sequential needs an index plus the ability to jump within the medium — tape doesn't support efficient jumping.

questions.stem: A web developer is important to a company because the web developer

options.A: is responsible for the company's website
options.B: assigns web addresses to all users in a company
options.C: backs up web files and stores the disks at another location
options.D: monitors the shutting down of competitors' websites

questions.explanation: A web developer designs, builds, and maintains the company's website — its layout, code, and functionality. The website is often the company's primary public face, so this role is essential.

distractors.B: Assigning addresses (URLs/IP addresses) is the job of network administrators or domain registrars, not web developers.
distractors.C: That describes a system administrator or backup technician.
distractors.D: Web developers don't monitor or attack competitor websites — that would be unethical and possibly illegal.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the LEAST reliable instruction for keeping passwords secure to resolve accounts security?

options.A: Use only long and strong passwords.
options.B: Change passwords on a regular basis.
options.C: Keep a copy of all passwords on your computer.
options.D: Create passwords with numbers and special characters.

questions.explanation: Storing your passwords as a plain file on your own computer defeats the purpose: if the computer is hacked, lost, or someone gets local access, every account is compromised at once. The other three are all good practices.

distractors.A: Long, strong passwords resist brute-force attacks — this IS reliable advice.
distractors.B: Periodic password changes limit damage if a password is leaked — reliable advice.
distractors.D: Including numbers and special characters increases entropy and makes guessing harder — reliable advice.

questions.stem: Which of the following examples describe a denial-of-service (DOS) attack? I. A sudden excessive increase in the number of junk emails II. Inability to reach regular websites because of too many requests III. Decrease in network performance because of flood of transactions
questions.explanation: A DoS attack overwhelms a service with more requests than it can handle, blocking legitimate users (II and III). Junk email is usually spam — annoying but not a DoS unless specifically targeting an email server with the intent to bring it down. CSEC treats spam and DoS as separate categories.

distractors.A: Includes I (junk email/spam), which is not the standard CSEC example of DoS.
distractors.B: Includes I and excludes II — but II is a textbook DoS example.
distractors.D: Includes I — but spam is not the standard DoS example in CSEC IT.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the protocol used to ensure that all web pages and servers communicate with each other?
questions.explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the standard for web pages — every browser request and web server response uses it. The secure variant is HTTPS.

distractors.A: IP routes packets between hosts but doesn't define how web pages are exchanged.
distractors.B: FTP transfers files between computers — used by web developers but not by browsers loading pages.
distractors.C: VoIP is for voice communication, not web pages.

questions.stem: Which of the following is true of accessing files sequentially?

options.C: Starts with the largest data field
options.D: Starts with the smallest data field

questions.explanation: Sequential access reads records from the first to the last in order. To reach the 50th record, you must pass through the previous 49.

distractors.A: That describes random/direct access — sequential is restrictive.
distractors.C: Starting with the largest data field doesn't make sense as a description of sequential access — sequential is about record order, not field size.
distractors.D: Same issue — sequential isn't defined by sorting on field size.

questions.stem: Which of the following machine-readable documents is NOT a turnaround document?

options.A: Lottery slips used to purchase lottery tickets
options.C: Fillable electronic survey forms completed online
options.D: Sheets used to record electric meter readings

questions.explanation: A turnaround document is a paper document with data printed by the system, filled in by a person, then returned to the system for processing. A purely electronic online survey never leaves the digital realm — there is no paper, so it can't be a turnaround.

distractors.A: A lottery slip is filled out and scanned — typical OMR turnaround.
distractors.B: MC answer sheets are pre-printed, marked, and read back — turnaround.
distractors.D: Meter reading sheets are printed by the company, completed by hand, returned for billing — classic turnaround.

questions.stem: Shaun's desktop computer in his home LAN suddenly loses the ability to access the Internet but his smartphone and other wireless devices continue to access the Internet using WiFi. Which of the following MOST likely caused the problem?

options.A: The modem is frozen and needs rebooting.
options.B: No service on ISP telecommunication line.
options.C: The computer and/or router needs rebooting.
options.D: The network cable is loose or needs replacing.

questions.explanation: The wireless devices still have Internet, so the modem, router and ISP line are all working. Only the wired desktop is offline — pointing to the wired link itself: a loose or faulty Ethernet cable.

distractors.A: If the modem were frozen, ALL devices including wireless ones would lose Internet.
distractors.B: If the ISP line were down, both the wireless devices and the desktop would lose access.
distractors.C: A failed router would knock out the WiFi too — not just the desktop.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT categorized as system software?
questions.explanation: Productivity tools (Microsoft Word, Excel, PowerPoint, etc.) are application software — programs that perform specific tasks for the user. The other three (utilities, translators, OS) are system software that manages or supports the computer itself.

distractors.A: Utilities (antivirus, disk cleanup, file managers) help maintain the system — system software.
distractors.B: Translators (compilers, interpreters, assemblers) convert source code into executable form — system software.
distractors.D: The operating system is the prime example of system software.

questions.stem: What is the order of connecting the hardware components of a LAN, starting from the ISP's telecommunication line, to a desktop computer?
questions.explanation: From the outside in: the ISP line enters the modem first (it converts the ISP signal into a usable form), then connects to the router (which manages traffic and assigns local addresses), and from the router to the switch (which extends to multiple devices). Finally, the desktop plugs into the switch.

distractors.B: Reversed order — the switch is at the end of the chain, not the beginning.
distractors.C: The modem must come first to handle the ISP signal — putting the router first won't work.
distractors.D: The router must come before the switch in this chain — the switch extends what the router provides.

questions.stem: A computer has these specifications: Intel Pentium IV, 64 bit, 3.6 GHz, 2 GB RAM, 160 GB SATA HDD, 7200 rpm. The CPU speed of the computer is
questions.explanation: CPU clock speed is measured in GHz (gigahertz) — billions of cycles per second. 3.6 GHz means the CPU performs 3.6 billion clock cycles per second.

distractors.A: 2 GB RAM is the size of memory, not CPU speed.
distractors.B: 64-bit refers to the CPU's architecture (data width per cycle), not its speed.
distractors.C: 7200 rpm is the rotational speed of the hard drive, not the CPU.

questions.stem: Using the same computer specifications as the previous question (Intel Pentium IV, 64 bit, 3.6 GHz, 2 GB RAM, 160 GB SATA HDD, 7200 rpm), the speed of the hard drive is
questions.explanation: Hard drive speed (for spinning platters) is measured in rpm (revolutions per minute). 7200 rpm is a common speed for desktop HDDs.

distractors.A: 2 GB is a memory size measurement.
distractors.B: 64-bit refers to CPU architecture, not hard drive speed.
distractors.D: 3.6 GHz is CPU clock speed.

questions.stem: In the URL  http://www.shoes.com,  'shoes.com' represents the
questions.explanation: 'shoes.com' is the domain name — the unique address that identifies the website on the Internet. shoes is the second-level domain and .com is the top-level domain (TLD).

distractors.A: The 'extension' (TLD) is just .com, not the whole domain name.
distractors.C: 'shoes' may be a company's chosen name, but technically the term is 'domain name'.
distractors.D: 'Internet company' is too vague — it's not a technical term in URL anatomy.

questions.stem: Which of the following symbols MUST be placed in front of the column letter and row number to create an absolute reference?
questions.explanation: The dollar sign ($) locks a cell reference. $A$1 is fully absolute — it stays exactly the same when copied. $A1 locks the column only; A$1 locks the row only.

distractors.A: % is the percentage operator, not used for cell references.
distractors.B: + is for addition or as a prefix to indicate positive numbers.
distractors.D: - is for subtraction or to indicate negative numbers.

questions.stem: Which of the following represents a correctly formatted e-mail address?
questions.explanation: A valid e-mail address has the form local-part@domain.tld. micah@loolu.net has a name (micah), an @ symbol, a domain (loolu) and a TLD (.net).

distractors.B: Begins with @ — there's no name before the @.
distractors.C: 'net' alone has no second-level domain — should be something like 'domain.net'.
distractors.D: Uses a colon (:) instead of a period or other valid character — invalid.

questions.stem: Selecting or highlighting a paragraph creates a
questions.explanation: In word processing, a selected (highlighted) section of text is called a block. Operations like cut, copy, paste, format and delete typically work on the current block.

distractors.A: A file is the saved document on disk — not a selection.
distractors.C: Merge combines documents (like in mail merge), unrelated to selection.
distractors.D: A document is the entire file — selecting a paragraph creates a block within the document.

questions.stem: The following formula is located in position B6 of a spreadsheet: = B3 + B4 + $B$5. What would be the resulting formula if the initial formula was copied to position located immediately to the right?
questions.explanation: Copying right by one column makes relative references shift from B to C. The absolute reference $B$5 stays exactly the same. So =B3+B4+$B$5 becomes =C3+C4+$B$5.

distractors.A: This treats $B$5 as a relative reference, which is wrong — the dollar signs lock it.
distractors.B: This shifts left, not right.
distractors.C: This shifts left, not right (and changes the absolute incorrectly).

questions.stem: In creating a query, a student entered the following criteria for the date field: >= 05/11/2022 and <= 05/14/2022. The output will show days
questions.explanation: Reading the dates as MM/DD/YYYY: 05/11/2022 = May 11, and 05/14/2022 = May 14. The range '≥ May 11 AND ≤ May 14' gives May 11 through May 14, 2022.

distractors.A: That excludes May 11 to May 14, which is exactly what's included.
distractors.C: That's the OPPOSITE — those are the dates excluded by the criteria.
distractors.D: That misreads the dates as DD/MM/YYYY (Nov 5 and Apr 14).

questions.stem: Which of the following is used for executing HTML code?
questions.explanation: A web browser (Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari) reads HTML, CSS and JavaScript and renders them as visible web pages. The browser is the program that 'executes' the HTML.

distractors.B: A hyperlink is a clickable link in an HTML page — it doesn't execute the HTML itself.
distractors.C: A web server stores and SENDS HTML files; it doesn't render them.
distractors.D: A URL is the address that tells the browser WHERE to fetch the HTML — but doesn't execute it.

questions.stem: Real numbers entered in a cell on a spreadsheet are automatically
questions.explanation: By default, numerical values entered into a spreadsheet cell are right-justified (so columns of numbers line up by units, tens, hundreds) and any decimal places typed in are preserved.

distractors.A: Decimal places are kept — 'no decimal places' is wrong.
distractors.C: Text is left-justified by default; numbers are right-justified.
distractors.D: Centered isn't the default for any data type in spreadsheets.

questions.stem: What type of cell addressing must be used to keep the cell address in a formula the same, when the cell address is copied to another cell?
questions.explanation: Absolute addressing (using the $ symbol, e.g., $A$1) locks the cell reference so it never changes when the formula is copied.

distractors.A: 'Fixed' is not the standard term in CSEC IT — the proper term is 'absolute'.
distractors.B: 'Direct' isn't a recognised cell addressing mode in spreadsheets.
distractors.C: Relative addressing changes when copied — the opposite of what's needed.

questions.stem: When testing an algorithm for correctness, which of the following methods is MOST effective in verifying results obtained when the data is keyed in?

options.B: Having the text in parts of words ignored
options.C: Finding each exact occurrence of the word
options.D: Finding the word using 'whole words only'

questions.explanation: Stepping through each occurrence individually (clicking 'Find Next' / 'Replace' for every match) is least efficient — it requires manual judgment per match. The other options use automated filters (whole-word, exact match) that handle bulk replacement efficiently.

distractors.B: Ignoring text in parts of words is exactly what 'whole words only' does — efficient.
distractors.C: Find each exact occurrence is automatic and quick — efficient.
distractors.D: 'Whole words only' is the standard efficient way to avoid replacing 'hen' inside 'then'.

questions.stem: The following spreadsheet has names, dates, gender (col D), exercise numbers (col G), and an exercise lookup table (A18:C22) with codes 010 Zumba 500, 020 Aerobics 400, 030 Spin 800, 040 Boxing 600, 050 Kick Boxing 800. Which of the following formulas will display the exercise name 'Zumba' in any cell where the formula is placed?
questions.explanation: VLOOKUP looks up the value in the first argument (here a cell from column G) inside the table and returns column 2. To display 'Zumba', the lookup cell must contain 010. Per the original paper's data, only G5 contains the exercise number 010.

distractors.A: G4 contains a different exercise number, so this returns a different exercise name.
distractors.C: G2 contains a header value, not an exercise number — would return an error or wrong result.
distractors.D: G10 contains a different exercise number — returns a different name.

questions.stem: Which of the following formulas will count the number of customers whose gender is equal to M in column D?
questions.explanation: COUNTIF(range, criteria) counts cells in the range that match the criteria. =COUNTIF(D2:D10, "M") counts every cell containing 'M' in the range D2 to D10.

distractors.A: COUNTID is not a real function.
distractors.B: COUNTA counts non-empty cells but does NOT take a criteria argument — passing 'M' is invalid syntax.
distractors.D: This passes D2 and D10 as separate arguments instead of a single range D2:D10 — invalid syntax.

questions.stem: Which of the following features allows the user to insert individual names in a primary document?
questions.explanation: Mail merge combines a single template (the primary document) with a list of names/addresses (the data source) to produce personalised copies for each entry — without retyping the template every time.

distractors.A: A footer is the bottom margin of a page; it doesn't pull names from a list.
distractors.B: A header is the top margin; it can hold a fixed name or page info, but isn't designed for personalisation.
distractors.D: Search and replace swaps one bit of text for another — but cannot pull individual names from a separate list.

questions.stem: When creating a database for a supermarket, the MOST appropriate data type to use for the price of an item in stock is
questions.explanation: The currency data type stores monetary values, automatically formatting with the currency symbol and the standard two decimal places. Best fit for prices.

distractors.A: Text would prevent numerical operations like totals or sorting by price.
distractors.B: Plain number could work, but currency is purpose-built for prices and handles formatting.
distractors.D: Autonumber generates unique sequential IDs — for primary keys, not prices.

questions.stem: The field that uniquely identifies a record in a database is called the
questions.explanation: The primary key is a field (or combination) whose value is unique for every record — used to identify each record uniquely. Examples: Student ID, ISBN, account number.

distractors.A: A foreign key in one table references the primary key of another table — used to LINK tables, not to uniquely identify within a table.
distractors.C: An alternate key is a candidate key not chosen as primary — a backup unique field.
distractors.D: 'Secondary key' is sometimes used informally for a non-primary index, but it's not the field that uniquely identifies records.

questions.stem: Which of the following characteristics must two database tables have in order for them to be linked?

options.B: The same data in all tuples
options.C: At least two tuples in the table
options.D: At least one common attribute name

questions.explanation: Two tables are linked through a common attribute (a foreign key in one table referencing the primary key of the other). The shared attribute is the bridge.

distractors.A: A composite key uses multiple fields together as a primary key — not required for two tables to link.
distractors.B: Tables don't need identical data — they just need a shared connecting field.
distractors.C: The number of records (tuples) is irrelevant to whether tables can be linked.

questions.stem:
The following algorithm is given:
Read (A, B)
IF A < B THEN print(A)

Which of the following tasks does the algorithm carry out?

options.A: Reads two numbers and prints the first number
options.B: Reads two numbers and prints the smaller number
options.C: Reads two numbers and prints the first number if it is smaller than the second number
options.D: Reads two numbers and prints the first number if it is larger than the second number

questions.explanation: The pseudocode reads A and B, then prints A only when A < B. This precisely matches: 'prints the first number if it is smaller than the second'.

distractors.A: This is unconditional — the actual code only prints when the condition holds.
distractors.B: Prints the smaller — but only when A is the smaller. If B is smaller, the code prints nothing, so this isn't fully accurate.
distractors.D: Reverses the condition — the code uses '<', which is 'smaller than', not 'larger than'.

questions.stem: You have been asked to write a program to calculate and print the seven times table from 1 to 12. Which FOR statement below has the correct high-level language syntax to accomplish the task?
questions.explanation: The seven times table from 1 to 12 needs 12 iterations: 1×7, 2×7, … 12×7. FOR count = 1 to 12 DO ... ENDFOR runs the loop body for count values 1 through 12, with proper open and close keywords.

distractors.A: Starts at 0 — would give a 0×7 row that wasn't asked for.
distractors.B: DOWNTO 12 starting from 1 makes no sense — DOWNTO requires the start value to be larger than the end value.
distractors.C: 12 DOWNTO 1 reverses the order — but more importantly, the question asks for 1 to 12 upward.

questions.stem: When implementing a program, which of the following steps comes after executing?
questions.explanation: The standard program development cycle ends with maintenance — after the program is up and running, you fix bugs, adapt to new requirements, and improve performance over its lifetime.

distractors.A: Linking happens BEFORE execution — combines compiled object files into the executable.
distractors.B: Compiling also happens before execution — turns source code into object code.
distractors.D: Interpreting (running source line-by-line) is an alternative to compiling — happens during execution, not after.

questions.stem: The MOST appropriate data type for storing the amount of interest earned on a sum of money is
questions.explanation: Interest amounts have decimal places (e.g., $12.45). The real (floating-point) data type stores decimal numbers and is the appropriate choice.

distractors.B: char stores a single character (a letter or digit) — not a number.
distractors.C: string stores text — you couldn't perform arithmetic.
distractors.D: integer stores whole numbers only — you'd lose all the decimal precision (lost cents).

questions.stem: Which of the following terms is an invalid variable name?
questions.explanation: Variable names cannot start with a digit. 2count begins with '2', so it's invalid in virtually every programming language.

distractors.A: Sum is fine — starts with a letter, no special characters.
distractors.C: Val15 is fine — starts with letter, contains a digit later (which is allowed).
distractors.D: Count is fine — same rules as Sum.

questions.stem: The decision box of a flow chart shows the
questions.explanation: The decision box (drawn as a diamond) holds a yes/no test or condition — for example, x > 10? The flow then branches based on the result.

distractors.A: Linking to another page is shown by an off-page connector symbol — not a decision box.
distractors.C: Calculations go inside a process box (rectangle), not a decision box.
distractors.D: Input/output uses the parallelogram symbol, not the diamond.

questions.stem: A computer programmer is coding an algorithm which calculates the class average for a given exam. He creates a variable called class_avg. Which data type should he specify when declaring this variable?
questions.explanation: An average can have decimals — for instance, the class average might be 73.5. Real (floating-point) is therefore the right data type.

distractors.B: Integer would round 73.5 down to 73, losing precision.
distractors.C: Boolean only stores TRUE/FALSE — useless for storing a number.
distractors.D: Character stores a single letter or digit — can't store a decimal number.

questions.stem:
Claude was asked to write a pseudocode that will accept three numbers from the user, find and display their average. He wrote:
Step 1: Start
Step 2: Read n1, n2, n3
Step 3: ?
Step 4: Write average
Step 5: Stop

Which of the following statements should be placed at Step 3?
questions.explanation: The mean of three numbers is their sum divided by 3: (n1 + n2 + n3) / 3.

distractors.A: Multiplies the numbers — that's a product, not an average.
distractors.B: Adds without dividing — that gives a sum, not an average.
distractors.C: Divides by 2 — that would give the average of two numbers, not three.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a conditional construct?
questions.explanation: IF...THEN...ELSE is the classic conditional construct — it picks one of two branches based on a Boolean test. The others are loop (iteration) constructs.

distractors.A: REPEAT...UNTIL is a post-test loop — iteration, not selection.
distractors.B: FOR...ENDWHILE has mismatched keywords (a real FOR pairs with ENDFOR), and FOR is for iteration anyway.
distractors.D: WHILE...ENDWHILE is a pre-test loop — iteration, not selection.

questions.stem: When testing an algorithm for correctness, which of the following methods is MOST effective in verifying results?
questions.explanation: A trace table systematically records the value of every variable at every step of the algorithm. It catches errors that a high-level walk-through misses — making it the most rigorous method.

distractors.B: A desk check is a less formal review of the code without tracking each variable change.
distractors.C: Test data feeds inputs and checks the final outputs, but doesn't catch where things go wrong inside the algorithm.
distractors.D: A dry run is a general manual walk-through without the systematic record-keeping of a trace table.

questions.stem: Which of the following are considered computer hardware?
questions.explanation: Output devices (monitor, printer, speakers) are physical components — they are hardware. Data, programs, and operating systems are all software/information.

distractors.A: Data is information stored in the computer; not a physical device.
distractors.B: Programs are software — sequences of instructions, not hardware.
distractors.D: Operating systems are software (Windows, macOS, Linux).

questions.stem: To use a program stored on a flash drive, the program must be transferred into
questions.explanation: The CPU can only execute instructions that are in RAM. When you launch a program, the OS loads it from the flash drive (secondary storage) into RAM (primary memory) for execution.

distractors.A: ROM is read-only, holds firmware (BIOS); programs aren't loaded there.
distractors.C: PROM is programmable read-only memory — also for firmware.
distractors.D: EPROM is erasable PROM — still read-only memory, not for running programs.

questions.stem: Which of the following operations is considered a feature of a biometric system?
questions.explanation: Biometrics use unique biological traits (fingerprints, retinas, voice, face) for identification. Matching fingerprints is the classic example.

distractors.B: Detecting heartbeats is a medical function, not biometric identification.
distractors.C: Diagnosing symptoms is medical, not biometric.
distractors.D: That's a biological/medical action, not technological identification.

questions.stem: Which of the following would NOT be considered cloud storage?

options.B: Data stored and accessible on a LAN
options.C: Data back-up and storage devices on the Internet
options.D: Data managed by storage providers or hosting companies

questions.explanation: Cloud storage means data on remote servers accessed over the Internet. A LAN is a private local network, not the cloud — that's local storage, not cloud.

distractors.A: Remote servers via Internet — that's cloud.
distractors.C: Internet-based backup IS cloud storage.
distractors.D: Third-party storage providers (Google Drive, Dropbox, AWS) are exactly what cloud storage means.

questions.stem: Which of the following could NOT be classified as a cloud-based application?
questions.explanation: Windows OS is local software installed on your computer's hard drive — not a cloud application. The other three are services accessed via the Internet.

distractors.A: Gmail is cloud-based — emails stored on Google's servers.
distractors.B: Dropbox is cloud storage.
distractors.C: Google Drive is cloud storage.

questions.stem: The lights in a room turn on as soon as a person enters. Which of the following input devices is MOST likely used to control the lighting in the room?
questions.explanation: A motion sensor (PIR or similar) detects movement and triggers the lights automatically. This is a classic IoT/automation use of sensors.

distractors.B: A remote requires a person to press a button — not automatic on entry.
distractors.C: Scanners read documents/images, not motion.
distractors.D: A keyboard requires the person to type a command.

questions.stem: Which of the following devices is used to input data into your computer?
questions.explanation: A mouse sends position and click data to the computer — a clear input device.

distractors.B: A speaker outputs audio; it doesn't send data to the computer.
distractors.C: A USB flash drive is storage — primarily classified as storage, not input.
distractors.D: An external hard drive is also storage.

questions.stem: In which of the following interface types is the user required to remember instructions and syntax?
questions.explanation: A command-driven (command-line) interface requires the user to type exact commands and remember the syntax (e.g., cd /home, rm -f file.txt). One typo and the command fails.

distractors.A: Menu-driven interfaces present options to pick from — no memorisation needed.
distractors.B: GUIs use icons and pointing — visual, not memorisation-based.
distractors.C: There's no standard interface called 'computer screen' — distractor.

questions.stem: The MOST suitable printer for creating a model of the globe is
questions.explanation: A 3D printer builds physical 3-dimensional objects layer by layer using plastic, resin, or other materials. To make a physical globe model, you'd need a 3D printer.

distractors.B: Laser printers produce 2D printouts on paper.
distractors.C: Ink-jet printers produce 2D printouts on paper.
distractors.D: Thermal printers produce 2D printouts (used for receipts, labels).

questions.stem: Which of the following protocols is used to ensure that all web pages and servers communicate with each other?
questions.explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the standard for web pages — every browser request and web server response uses it.

distractors.A: IP routes packets between hosts but doesn't define how web pages are exchanged.
distractors.B: FTP transfers files between computers — not for browsers loading pages.
distractors.C: VoIP is for voice communication, not web pages.

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows a wireless symbol (concentric arcs). The symbol represents
questions.explanation: The classic Wi-Fi symbol shows concentric arcs spreading out from a central point — representing radio waves broadcasting outward from a wireless access point.

distractors.A: LAN is a network type, not represented by a single icon like this.
distractors.C: Hotspot uses similar imagery but the arcs-only icon is the standard Wi-Fi mark.
distractors.D: Bluetooth has its own distinctive 'B' rune-like logo.

questions.stem: Which of the following options shows an increase in computer memory capacity?
questions.explanation: Memory units in increasing order: KB < MB < GB < TB. MB → GB → TB shows a clear monotonic increase across three levels.

distractors.A: True increase but only one step (MB to GB).
distractors.C: This is a DECREASE, not an increase.
distractors.D: Order is jumbled — not a clean increase.

questions.stem: In the URL  http://www.shoes.com,  'shoes.com' stands for the
questions.explanation: 'shoes.com' is the domain name — the unique address that identifies the website on the Internet. shoes is the second-level domain and .com is the top-level domain (TLD).

distractors.A: The 'extension' (TLD) is just .com, not the whole domain name.
distractors.B: 'shoes' may be a company's chosen name, but the technical term is 'domain name'.
distractors.D: 'Internet company' is too vague — not a technical term.

questions.stem: Which of the following checks is NOT a validation technique?
questions.explanation: Double entry is a verification technique (entering data twice and comparing), not validation. Validation checks examine the data itself (range, reasonableness, format, etc.).

distractors.A: Range check is validation — checks if value falls in an allowed range.
distractors.C: Inconsistency check is validation — looks for conflicting fields.
distractors.D: Reasonableness check is validation — flags unreasonable values.

questions.stem: Which of the following types of technology is commonly used by banks to read data on cheques?
questions.explanation: MICR (Magnetic Ink Character Recognition) reads the special magnetic-ink digits printed at the bottom of cheques — the routing number and account number. The magnetic property makes them readable even when the cheque is dirty or marked.

distractors.A: POS terminals read credit/debit cards, not cheques.
distractors.B: OCR reads ordinary printed text — too unreliable for cheque security.
distractors.C: OME isn't a standard CSEC abbreviation.

questions.stem: Transferring data from a remote computer to your computer is referred to as
questions.explanation: Downloading = pulling data FROM a remote computer TO yours. Uploading is the opposite (pushing data from your computer to a remote server).

distractors.A: Browsing is moving between web pages.
distractors.B: Uploading is the opposite direction — sending data to a remote computer.
distractors.C: Data mining is analysis of large datasets to find patterns.

questions.stem: Data that has been processed and that has meaning is termed
questions.explanation: Raw data becomes information when it has been processed and given meaning. The classic CSEC IT formula: data + meaning = information.

distractors.B: A database is a structured store of data — but data inside a database can still be raw.
distractors.C: Storage is where data is kept; doesn't imply meaning.
distractors.D: Output can be data or information; the term 'information' is more specific.

questions.stem: Telecommuting can BEST be described as

options.C: working from home by connecting home and office
options.D: working from the office after establishing a connection at home

questions.explanation: Telecommuting = working from home (or another remote location) by using telecommunications to connect to the office network. Saves the daily commute.

distractors.A: Using a computer while commuting isn't telecommuting — it's just working in transit.
distractors.B: Phoning while commuting isn't telecommuting either.
distractors.D: Working from the office is normal commuting, not telecommuting.

questions.stem: The process of scrambling messages at the sending end and unscrambling them at the receiving end is called
questions.explanation: Encryption converts plaintext into unreadable ciphertext using a mathematical algorithm and key. The receiver decrypts using the matching key. This is the foundation of secure communication.

distractors.A: Coding can mean writing programs or using ciphers — too vague.
distractors.B: Espionage is spying — what eavesdroppers do, not the protection against it.
distractors.D: Eavesdropping is the attack encryption defends against, not the protective process.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a hardware component of a computer system?
questions.explanation: A data file is software/information stored on hardware. The hardware itself is the storage device — the file is just bits arranged in a particular pattern. The other three options are physical hardware.

distractors.B: Main memory (RAM) is hardware — physical chips on the motherboard.
distractors.C: Peripheral devices (mouse, keyboard, printer) are hardware.
distractors.D: The CPU is hardware — the central processor chip.

questions.stem: There are many software restrictions used by computers to protect data and maintain data integrity. Which of the following entities are software restriction media? I. Viruses II. Passwords III. Encryptions
questions.explanation: Passwords (II) restrict access. Encryption (III) protects data even if accessed. Viruses (I) are THREATS to data integrity, not protections — they're the opposite of restrictions.

distractors.A: Includes viruses — those are threats, not protections.
distractors.B: Includes viruses — those are threats.
distractors.D: Includes viruses — those are threats.

questions.stem: Which of the following devices can be used for both input and output?
questions.explanation: A touchscreen displays output (the screen image) AND accepts input (touch coordinates). Same device, both functions.

distractors.B: A barcode reader only reads (input).
distractors.C: A flatbed scanner only scans (input).
distractors.D: A document reader only reads (input).

questions.stem: Which of the following is a hardware user interface?
questions.explanation: A touchscreen is a physical hardware device that the user interacts with directly — both displaying output and accepting input. It's a hardware-based user interface.

distractors.A: A GUI (Graphical User Interface) is software, not hardware.
distractors.B: An icon is a graphical element on a GUI — software, not hardware.
distractors.C: Sensors detect environmental data, not typically classified as a user interface.

questions.stem: An official of Company X sells confidential information about his company to Company Y. What is the term used for this type of information misuse?
questions.explanation: Industrial espionage is the theft or unauthorised disclosure of business secrets — trade secrets, strategic plans, customer lists — usually for commercial gain.

distractors.A: Identity theft is stealing personal details (name, ID, bank info) — different scope.
distractors.B: Computer fraud involves manipulating computers for financial gain — broader and not specifically about confidential business info.
distractors.D: Electronic eavesdropping is real-time interception, not deliberate disclosure by an insider.

questions.stem: Which part of the central processing unit (CPU) coordinates its activities?
questions.explanation: The Control Unit (CU) directs the operations of the CPU. It tells the ALU and registers what to do, when to do it, and where to send the results.

distractors.A: Registers are tiny, fast storage locations within the CPU — they hold data being worked on, but don't coordinate.
distractors.C: Main memory is RAM — outside the CPU.
distractors.D: The ALU performs arithmetic and logic — it does the work, not the coordinating.

questions.stem: Given a computer with these specifications: Intel Pentium IV / 64 bit, 3.6 GHz, 2 GiB RAM, 160 GB SATA HDD, 7200 rpm, 533 MHz. The processing speed of the central processing unit (CPU) is
questions.explanation: CPU clock speed is measured in GHz (gigahertz). 3.6 GHz means the CPU performs 3.6 billion clock cycles per second.

distractors.A: 2 GiB RAM is the size of memory, not CPU speed.
distractors.C: 7200 rpm is the rotational speed of the hard drive.
distractors.D: 533 MHz is the bus speed (memory bus or front-side bus), not the CPU clock.

questions.stem: The part of a private network using Internet technology and public telecommunications that can be accessed by business suppliers, vendors, customers and partners is called
questions.explanation: An extranet extends a private intranet to authorised external parties — suppliers, partners, and customers — over the Internet.

distractors.A: The Internet is fully public — not private at all.
distractors.B: An intranet is private and restricted to people within the organisation only.
distractors.D: Ethernet is a networking technology, not a type of network with a specific user-base.

questions.stem: Which of the following pairs represents hardware devices that act as user interfaces?
questions.explanation: The keyboard is the standard input UI; the monitor is the standard output UI. Together they form the most common pair of hardware user interfaces in everyday computing.

distractors.B: Optical and hard drives are storage devices, not user interfaces.
distractors.C: Character readers are input devices but not the typical 'user interface' pair.
distractors.D: While touchscreen is a UI, this pair is less universal than keyboard/monitor.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a correctly formatted e-mail address?
questions.explanation: A valid e-mail address has the form local-part@domain.tld. lana@happy.net has a name (lana), an @ symbol, a domain (happy) and a TLD (.net).

distractors.B: Begins with @ — there's no name before the @.
distractors.C: 'net' alone has no second-level domain (need domain.tld after @).
distractors.D: Has two @ symbols — only one is allowed.

questions.stem: Which of the following types of software should be used for applications that involve repetitive numerical calculations?
questions.explanation: Spreadsheets are designed for repetitive numerical calculations — formulas, functions, and computations propagate across cells automatically.

distractors.A: Databases store and query data, but aren't optimised for arithmetic across cells.
distractors.B: Presentation software creates slides — not for calculations.
distractors.D: Word processors handle text, not calculations.

questions.stem: The following formula is located in position B6 of a spreadsheet: = B3 + B4 + $B$5. If the formula is copied to the position located immediately to the LEFT, what would be the resulting formula?
questions.explanation: Copying LEFT one column makes relative references shift from B to A. The absolute reference $B$5 stays exactly the same. So =B3+B4+$B$5 becomes =A3+A4+$B$5.

distractors.A: This treats $B$5 as relative — wrong.
distractors.B: This treats $B$5 as relative as well.
distractors.D: This shifts right, not left.

questions.stem: The following table contains data on three countries (A, B, C) for 2020 and 2021. Which of the following types of charts is LEAST suitable for representing the data above?
questions.explanation: A pie chart shows parts of a single whole — it isn't suitable for comparing across multiple categories OR multiple time points. With three countries × two years, you'd need either many pies or a bar/column/line chart.

distractors.B: Bar charts work well for comparing values across categories — suitable here.
distractors.C: Line charts can show changes between 2020 and 2021 — suitable.
distractors.D: Column charts are essentially vertical bar charts — suitable.

diagram.alt: Table of country data for 2020 and 2021

questions.stem: Spreadsheet formulae use
questions.explanation: Spreadsheet formulas operate on values (literals like 42), ranges (e.g., A1:A10) and cell addresses (e.g., B5). Labels (text) are usually not used as formula operands directly.

distractors.A: Labels aren't typical formula contents — they're text.
distractors.B: Labels aren't typical formula contents.
distractors.D: Includes labels, which aren't standard formula contents.

questions.stem: Which of the following alignment buttons can be used to fully justify text?
questions.explanation: Full justification aligns text to BOTH the left and right margins by adjusting word spacing. The icon shows lines that all reach the same length on both sides — flush on both edges.

distractors.A: Left alignment leaves the right side ragged.
distractors.B: Centered text has both sides ragged but visually balanced.
distractors.C: Right alignment leaves the left side ragged.

questions.stem: In creating a query, a student entered the following criteria for the date field: >= 05/11/2021 and <= 05/14/2021. The output will show days
questions.explanation: Reading the dates as MM/DD/YYYY: 05/11/2021 = May 11, and 05/14/2021 = May 14. The range '≥ May 11 AND ≤ May 14' gives May 11 through May 14, 2021.

distractors.A: That excludes May 11 to May 14, which is exactly what's included.
distractors.C: That's the OPPOSITE — those are the dates excluded.
distractors.D: That misreads the dates as DD/MM/YYYY.

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows a database table with Item No., Item Desc. and Quantity columns: H050 Hammer 20, H040 Hammer 40, FK030 Fork 35, FK020 Fork 40, FK015 Fork 45. The database table above is sorted by
questions.explanation: Item Numbers in this order: H050 → H040 → FK030 → FK020 → FK015. Both within the H-prefixed and FK-prefixed groups, the numbers DECREASE. So the table is sorted by Item No. in descending order.

distractors.A: Quantities (20, 40, 35, 40, 45) aren't in ascending order.
distractors.B: Quantities aren't in descending order either (e.g., 35 → 40 jumps up).
distractors.C: That would mean items go from smallest to largest number, but they're going largest to smallest.

diagram.alt: Database table with five rows of items showing Item No., Item Desc. and Quantity

questions.stem: Which of the following sequences of steps for deleting a record in a database table is correct? I. Choose the delete option. II. Select the row to be deleted and right click. III. Open table in design view. IV. Choose yes to confirm deletion.
questions.explanation: The logical order: III (open the table) → II (select the target row + right click) → I (choose Delete from the context menu) → IV (confirm Yes when asked).

distractors.A: Starts with the delete command before the table is even open.
distractors.C: Has selecting the row before opening the table.
distractors.D: Has confirmation BEFORE actually choosing delete.

questions.stem: Which of the following could identify a hyperlink in an HTML document? I. A link to an email address II. A link to another webpage III. A link to another location within the webpage
questions.explanation: All three are valid hyperlinks. Email links use mailto:. Web page links use the URL. Within-page links use anchors (#section-id). HTML's <a href> handles all three.

distractors.A: Excludes within-page anchors, which ARE valid hyperlinks.
distractors.B: Excludes external page links — also valid.
distractors.C: Excludes email links — also valid.

questions.stem: Which of the following content controls would be useful when creating a fillable electronic form for online use? I. Date picker II. Check box III. Drop-down list
questions.explanation: All three are common form controls: date pickers for dates, check boxes for yes/no choices, drop-down lists for selecting from predefined options. Most online forms use all three.

distractors.A: Drop-downs are also useful — they're the standard way to limit input to predefined options.
distractors.B: Check boxes are also useful — for yes/no or multiple selection.
distractors.C: Date pickers are also useful — for date input.

questions.stem: Which of the following sequences of steps is required to centre a range of cells in a spreadsheet?

options.A: Select and copy the text across the cells.
options.B: Select and centre the text and then merge the cells.
options.C: Select the text across the cells, then merge and centre.
options.D: Type the text in the middle of the selected cells.

questions.explanation: The standard 'merge and centre' workflow: Select the range of cells, then click Merge & Centre — which combines the cells and centres the content in one step.

distractors.A: Copying doesn't centre anything.
distractors.B: Centering text alone keeps individual cells separate — you also need to merge to get a single centered cell.
distractors.D: You can't 'type in the middle' — text fills from the left in spreadsheet cells.

questions.stem: The following algorithm is given: Read (A, B). If A < B Then Print(A). Which of the following describes the algorithm?

options.A: Reads two numbers and prints the first number
options.B: Reads two numbers and prints the smaller number
options.C: Reads two numbers and prints the first number if it is larger than the second number
options.D: Reads two numbers and prints the first number if it is smaller than the second number

questions.explanation: The pseudocode reads A and B, then prints A only when A < B. This precisely matches: 'prints the first number if it is smaller than the second'.

distractors.A: Unconditional — but the actual code only prints when the condition holds.
distractors.B: Prints the smaller — but only when A is the smaller. If B is smaller, the code prints nothing.
distractors.C: REVERSED — the code uses '<', meaning 'smaller than', not 'larger than'.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a high-level programming language?
questions.explanation: Assembly is a low-level programming language. It's a direct, human-readable representation of machine code instructions, very close to the hardware.

distractors.A: C is a high-level language (compiled to machine code).
distractors.B: Java is a high-level language (runs on the JVM).
distractors.C: Pascal is a high-level language.

questions.stem: The decision box of a program flowchart shows the
questions.explanation: The decision box (drawn as a diamond) holds a yes/no test or condition — for example, x > 10? The flow then branches based on the result.

distractors.A: Linking to another page is shown by an off-page connector symbol.
distractors.C: Calculations go inside a process box (rectangle).
distractors.D: Input/output uses the parallelogram symbol.

questions.stem: The following algorithm is given: I. Ask users for length and width II. length, width III. area ← length * width IV. "Area of rectangle is", area. Which of the following options, in the correct order, can be used to replace I, II and III in the algorithm?
questions.explanation: The algorithm structure: first prompt the user (ask for input), then read the input, then process (calculate area). So I=Prompt, II=Read, III=Process.

distractors.A: Has Read first — but you must prompt before reading.
distractors.B: Has Process first — but you must read inputs before processing them.
distractors.D: Has Prompt only third — but prompting comes first in this flow.

questions.stem: What is the name given to an identifier that always holds the same value?
questions.explanation: A constant is a named identifier whose value is fixed at the time of declaration and cannot be changed during program execution. Examples: pi = 3.14159, max_students = 30.

distractors.C: A variable's value CAN change — that's why it's called a 'variable'.
distractors.D: Storage is the general concept of where data is held — not a specific identifier type.

questions.stem:
The following algorithm is given:

(3) Vat ← price * 15%
(4) Write Vat

Which line contains a processing statement?
questions.explanation: A processing statement performs computation. Line 3 calculates Vat by multiplying price by 15%. Lines 1 and 4 are output (Write); Line 2 is input (Read).

distractors.A: Line 1 is an output statement (prints a prompt).
distractors.B: Line 2 is an input statement (reads from user).
distractors.D: Line 4 is an output statement (writes the result).

questions.stem: Which of the following items are considered computer hardware?

questions.explanation: The CPU runs programs from RAM.

questions.stem: Which of the following sequences of steps is correct for deleting a field in a database table?

options.B: Open table in design view. Select row to be deleted and right click. Choose the delete row option. Choose yes to confirm deletion.

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows a database table with Item No., Item Desc., and Quantity (H050 Hammer 20, H040 Hammer 40, FK030 Fork 35, FK020 Fork 40, FK015 Fork 45). The database table above is sorted by

questions.stem: The following algorithm is given:  Read (A, B). If A < B Then Print(A). Which of the following tasks does the following algorithm perform?

questions.stem: The binary equivalent of the decimal number 7 is
questions.explanation:
To convert decimal 7 to binary, find the powers of 2 that sum to 7: 4 + 2 + 1 = 7. So binary is 111₂.

Place values: 4's = 1, 2's = 1, 1's = 1 → 111.

distractors.A: 100₂ = 4 in decimal — only the 4's place is on.
distractors.B: 101₂ = 4 + 0 + 1 = 5 in decimal.
distractors.C: 110₂ = 4 + 2 + 0 = 6 in decimal.

questions.stem: Which of the following tasks is NOT a function of the control unit (CU)?

options.C: Coordinating the operation of the internal registers
options.D: Controlling the sequencing of machine instructions

questions.explanation: Performing arithmetic operations is the job of the Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU), NOT the Control Unit. The Control Unit fetches instructions, decodes them, and coordinates the other CPU components — but it does not perform calculations itself.

distractors.B: Reading and interpreting machine instructions IS the role of the CU (instruction decode).
distractors.C: Coordinating internal registers (where the CPU holds data temporarily) IS a CU function.
distractors.D: Controlling instruction sequencing — fetch-decode-execute order — IS the CU's primary role.

questions.stem: The two's complement of a binary number is formed by

options.D: adding one to the one's complement

questions.explanation: The 2's complement is found in two steps: (1) Find the 1's complement (flip every bit — 0→1 and 1→0). (2) Add 1 to the result. This represents the negative of the original number in 2's complement notation.

distractors.A: Binary uses only 0 and 1 — there is no '2' in binary.
distractors.B: Same problem — 2 is not a binary digit.
distractors.C: Doing the 1's complement twice just gives back the original number — that's not 2's complement.

questions.stem: Which of the following terms represents a hexadecimal number?
questions.explanation: The subscript indicates the base of the number system. Subscript 16 = hexadecimal (uses digits 0-9 and letters A-F). So 5C₁₆ is a hex number.

distractors.B: Subscript 2 = binary.
distractors.C: Subscript 10 = decimal (everyday numbers).
distractors.D: Subscript 8 = octal.

questions.stem: Which of the following systems provides transmission of data in BOTH directions at the same time?
questions.explanation: Full duplex allows simultaneous two-way communication — like a telephone call where both parties can talk at the same time.

distractors.A: Simplex = one direction only (e.g., radio broadcast).
distractors.C: Half duplex = both directions but one at a time (e.g., walkie-talkie — push to talk, release to listen).
distractors.D: 'Null duplex' is not a recognised transmission mode.

questions.stem: Which of the following functions is performed by a modem?
questions.explanation: A modem (modulator-demodulator) converts digital signals from a computer into analog signals for transmission over phone lines (modulate), and converts incoming analog back to digital (demodulate).

distractors.B: Upload is moving data outward — that's a use, not what a modem fundamentally does.
distractors.C: Download is moving data inward — again a use, not the modem's defining function.
distractors.D: Upload/download are network operations performed using a modem, not the modem's conversion function itself.

questions.stem: Which of the following is detected by a biometric system?
questions.explanation: A biometric system identifies a person by a unique biological feature — fingerprint, retina, face, or voice. Matching fingerprints is the classic biometric operation.

distractors.B: Heart-rhythm monitoring is medical instrumentation (ECG/Holter), not authentication.
distractors.C: Diagnosing symptoms is a medical/AI function — possibly an expert system, not biometric.
distractors.D: Lubricating joints is a biological function performed by synovial fluid, nothing to do with computing.

questions.stem: The data from the form shown above (a multiple-choice bubble sheet being filled with a pencil) is captured by

distractors.A: USB is a port/cable standard, not a data-capture method.
distractors.B: OCR (Optical Character Recognition) reads typed/handwritten text characters — different from filled bubbles.

questions.stem: In Microsoft PowerPoint, a single page shown on a screen to display information is called a
questions.explanation: In PowerPoint, each individual page of content is called a slide. A collection of slides shown in sequence is a presentation.

questions.stem: Computer programming instructions like "Sort customer by last name" and "Total income by department" belong to which generation of programming language?
questions.explanation: Fourth-generation languages (4GL) use English-like, near-natural syntax aimed at non-programmers — typical of report-writers and database query languages (SQL, FoxPro, etc.). The given instructions read almost like plain English, which is the hallmark of 4GL.

distractors.A: First gen = machine code (raw 0s and 1s) — nothing like English.
distractors.B: Second gen = assembly language — abbreviated mnemonics like MOV, ADD.
distractors.C: Third gen = high-level languages like Pascal, C, Java — structured but still requires programming knowledge.

questions.stem: Which of the following devices produces a soft copy?
questions.explanation: A soft copy is an electronic display that disappears when the device is turned off — a monitor shows soft copies (text, images on screen).

distractors.B: A plotter also produces hard copy (large engineering/architectural drawings).
distractors.D: Microfilm is a hard-copy archival format — not soft.

questions.stem: The four-bit binary representation of decimal number 8 is
questions.explanation: Decimal 8 = 8 × 1 = 1000₂ in 4-bit binary. Place values for 4-bit: 8s, 4s, 2s, 1s. Only the 8s place is on, giving 1000.

distractors.A: 0001 = 1 in decimal.
distractors.B: 0010 = 2 in decimal.
distractors.C: 0100 = 4 in decimal.

questions.stem: Which of the following communication modes transmits signals between two connected devices in one direction only?
questions.explanation: Simplex is one-way only — like a TV or radio broadcast where information flows in one direction from sender to receiver, never the reverse.

distractors.A: Half duplex = both directions, but one at a time (e.g., walkie-talkie).
distractors.B: Broadband refers to a high-bandwidth data connection — not a transmission direction.
distractors.D: Full duplex = both directions simultaneously (e.g., phone call).

questions.stem: Brenda entered "2061" instead of "2016" in a form. What type of error does this represent?
questions.explanation: A transposition error happens when adjacent characters are swapped — "2016" → "2061" (the '6' and '1' have been swapped). Very common when typing quickly.

distractors.B: Typographical error is broader — any typing mistake. Transposition is the specific kind here.
distractors.C: Proofreading is the act of checking — not a type of error.
distractors.D: Spelling errors involve letters in words — "2016" isn't a word.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements is TRUE of the 3½″ floppy disk?

options.A: Pictures and text can be stored.
options.B: Storage capacity is typically around 1.44 MB.
options.C: Data can be accessed more quickly than from a hard drive.
options.D: Large amounts of data can be stored.

questions.explanation: The standard 3½″ floppy disk has a capacity of 1.44 MB — about enough for a few documents or a single small image. (HD floppies = 1.44 MB; DD = 720 KB.)

distractors.A: Pictures and text CAN be stored, but the question asks for what is most distinctively true. The 1.44 MB capacity is the defining feature.
distractors.C: Floppies are MUCH slower than hard drives, not faster.
distractors.D: 1.44 MB is tiny by modern standards — not large amounts.

questions.stem: Which of the following applications is BEST suited to batch processing?
questions.explanation: Batch processing handles a large volume of similar transactions at once, with no need for immediate response. Salary preparation is the textbook example — all employees' pay is calculated together at the end of the pay period.

distractors.B: Online gaming requires real-time interactive response — opposite of batch.
distractors.C: Flight simulation needs instant feedback — real-time.
distractors.D: Theatre booking must confirm seats immediately to avoid double-booking — real-time.

questions.stem: Given a stock-balance spreadsheet where Column C is "Stock In" and Column D is "Stock Out", which formula should be placed in Cell E2 to obtain the stock balance?
questions.explanation: Stock balance = Stock In − Stock Out. So =C2-D2.
